dialogues: You practiced equations with 2s, 5s, and 10s. Let's connect what you've learned.
You've read graphs before. Here's a scale of 2. 3 symbols, 3 groups of 2, that's 3 times 2 equals 6.
Same graph shape, but scale of 5. Same 3 symbols, but now 3 groups of 5, that's 3 times 5 equals 15.
And scale of 10. 3 groups of 10 is 3 times 10 equals 30.
Look what happened. Same graph. Same number of symbols. But when we changed the scale, the multiplication changed. The product got bigger. That's because we were multiplying by bigger numbers.
Here are all three patterns together. 2s are always even. 5s end in 0 or 5. 10s end in 0. These patterns aren't just facts. They're how you check your work.
Say you see this question: what is 8 times 5? And you have these choices.

Right. 13 ends in 3 and 42 ends in 2. Neither fits the 5s pattern, so they can't be right. That leaves 35 and 40. Both end in 0 or 5, so the pattern alone can't tell you which one. That's where skip-counting finishes the job.

Three ways to find 6 times 5 equals 30. You can look at the groups and count. You can skip-count: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30. Or you can add: 5 plus 5 plus 5 plus 5 plus 5 plus 5 equals 30. Same answer. All three work.
Which way made more sense to you today?
Right. Seeing the groups makes it real. You can count each one. And notice: counting those groups by 5s IS skip-counting. The picture and the counting go together.
That's right. Skip-counting is fast when you know the counts. That'll keep being useful.
You got it. Adding lets you see each group. As you add, you're also counting by 5s. That's a solid strategy too.
Before you even skip-count, just looking at 7 times 2, what do you know about the answer?
Right. You know it's even before counting a single number. That's the pattern working for you.
You've been practicing multiplication with 2, 5, and 10. You used patterns to check your work and predict answers. You connected picture graphs, skip-counting, and equations.
Those patterns are your toolkit. They help you work faster and check your thinking.
If you remember one thing from today, what would it be?
Right. Skip-counting is your go-to strategy for multiplication. It'll keep working as the factors change.
That's a solid takeaway. Pattern-checking catches mistakes before they stick. That's powerful.
Good insight. You've been multiplying longer than you realized. Graphs were the start.
Yes. That vocabulary gives you a way to talk about multiplication precisely.
You can compute products for 2s, 5s, and 10s. You know the patterns. You can build complete equations with factors and products.

Next time, you'll see equations where one of the factors is missing, like something times 5 equals 30. You'll figure out what's hiding. You're ready because you already know what these equations mean.
